concepts.name: Points, Lines & Planes
concepts.intuition_gloss: The bare bones of space: dots with no size, perfectly straight paths, and perfectly flat sheets extending forever.
concepts.formal_gloss: The undefined primitive terms of Euclidean geometry, related by incidence axioms such as 'two distinct points determine exactly one line'.
sources: Euclid, Elements Book I (definitions and postulates)

concepts.name: Angle
concepts.intuition_gloss: The amount of turn between two rays hinged at a common point.
concepts.formal_gloss: The figure formed by two rays with a shared endpoint, measured in degrees or radians by the fraction of a full rotation (or arc of a unit circle) it subtends.

concepts.name: Congruence
concepts.intuition_gloss: Two shapes are congruent if you could pick one up and set it down exactly on top of the other.
concepts.formal_gloss: Two figures are congruent iff some isometry maps one onto the other; for triangles, the SSS, SAS, and ASA criteria are equivalent tests.
sources: Common Core high-school geometry (transformational definition of congruence)

concepts.name: Similarity
concepts.intuition_gloss: Same shape, different size — one figure is a photographic enlargement of the other.
concepts.formal_gloss: Two figures are similar iff one is the image of the other under a similarity transformation (an isometry composed with a uniform dilation), giving equal angles and proportional lengths.

concepts.name: Area & Volume
concepts.intuition_gloss: How much flat space a shape covers, or how much room a solid fills, measured by tiling it with unit squares or cubes.
concepts.formal_gloss: A finitely additive, congruence-invariant measure on plane or solid regions, normalized so the unit square (or cube) has measure 1.

resources.title: But why is a sphere's surface area four times its shadow? (3Blue1Brown)
Khan Academy: Geometry (area and volume units)

sources: Euclid, Elements Books I-II, XII (method of exhaustion)
Hilbert's third problem (limits of dissection) as depth marker

concepts.name: Circle
concepts.intuition_gloss: All the points at one fixed distance from a center — the roundest shape, with the number pi hiding in its measurements.
concepts.formal_gloss: The locus {P : d(P, O) = r} for a center O and radius r, with circumference 2πr and enclosed area πr².

concepts.name: Polygons & Polyhedra
concepts.intuition_gloss: Shapes fenced by straight sides in the plane, and solids walled by flat faces in space — triangles to cubes to soccer balls.
concepts.formal_gloss: A polygon is a closed plane figure bounded by finitely many segments; a polyhedron is a solid bounded by finitely many polygonal faces; the regular convex cases are fully classified (n-gons; the five Platonic solids).

concepts.name: Pythagorean Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: In any right triangle, the square built on the longest side exactly fills the two squares built on the shorter sides.
concepts.formal_gloss: In a right triangle with legs a, b and hypotenuse c, a² + b² = c²; equivalently, Euclidean distance is the square root of the sum of squared coordinate differences.
sources: leverage: underlies Euclidean distance, norms, and inner products across the map

concepts.name: Euclidean Geometry
concepts.intuition_gloss: The classical geometry of flat space, where an entire edifice of shapes is deduced step by step from five stated rules.
concepts.formal_gloss: The geometry axiomatized by Euclid's five postulates (made rigorous by Hilbert's axioms), characterized among geometries by the parallel postulate.
sources: Princeton Companion to Mathematics (geometry survey chapters)

concepts.name: Compass & Straightedge Construction
concepts.intuition_gloss: Drawing exact figures with only a pencil, an unmarked ruler, and a compass — and discovering that some figures are forever out of reach.
concepts.formal_gloss: Figures generable from two given points by finitely many line and circle drawings; the constructible lengths form the closure of Q under square roots, whence trisecting the angle, doubling the cube, and squaring the circle are impossible.
sources: leverage: canonical bridge from geometry to abstract algebra

concepts.name: Trigonometry
concepts.intuition_gloss: Ratios in right triangles become functions that encode every rotation and every repeating wave.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of sine, cosine, and tangent, defined on the unit circle as coordinates of a rotated point, satisfying identities such as sin²θ + cos²θ = 1.
sources: leverage: gateway to Fourier analysis, complex exponentials, and all wave phenomena

concepts.name: Tessellation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Covering an endless floor with repeating tiles and no gaps — the geometry of wallpaper, honeycombs, and Escher prints.
concepts.formal_gloss: A covering of the plane by non-overlapping tiles; periodic tilings are classified by the 17 wallpaper groups, while aperiodic tile sets (Penrose) tile only without repetition.
sources: recreational-to-research pipeline: Penrose tilings, 2023 aperiodic monotile

concepts.name: Isometry (Rigid Motion)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Every way to move a shape without distorting it — slides, turns, and flips — and nothing else.
concepts.formal_gloss: A distance-preserving bijection of a metric space; the isometries of the Euclidean plane are exactly translations, rotations, reflections, and glide reflections, and they form a group.
sources: classification of plane isometries (standard undergraduate result)
leverage: makes congruence precise and connects geometry to group theory

concepts.name: Conic Sections
concepts.intuition_gloss: Slice a cone at different angles and you get circles, ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas — the paths traced by planets and cannonballs.
concepts.formal_gloss: The plane curves defined by degree-2 polynomial equations, classified up to isometry as ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas (plus degenerate cases).

concepts.name: Dimension
concepts.intuition_gloss: How many independent directions a space offers — a line has one, a tabletop two, our world three, and mathematics keeps climbing without stopping.
concepts.formal_gloss: The number of coordinates needed to specify a point; for vector spaces the cardinality of a basis, with topological and fractal (Hausdorff) generalizations.

resources.title: Flatland: A Romance of Many Dimensions (Abbott, Project Gutenberg)

concepts.name: Non-Euclidean Geometry
concepts.intuition_gloss: Perfectly consistent worlds where parallel lines can meet or fan apart, and triangle angles refuse to sum to 180 degrees.
concepts.formal_gloss: Geometries satisfying Euclid's axioms except the parallel postulate: elliptic/spherical (no parallels) and hyperbolic (infinitely many), realized on surfaces of constant positive or negative curvature.

resources.title: How One Line in the Oldest Math Text Hinted at Hidden Universes (Veritasium)

sources: leverage: historic proof that axioms are choices, feeding into modern logic and Riemannian geometry

concepts.name: Convexity
concepts.intuition_gloss: A shape with no dents: the straight path between any two of its points never leaves it.
concepts.formal_gloss: A set C in a real vector space is convex if tx + (1−t)y ∈ C for all x, y ∈ C and t ∈ [0,1], supporting separation, Helly, and extreme-point theorems.
sources: leverage: the dividing line between tractable and intractable optimization

concepts.name: Fractal Geometry
concepts.intuition_gloss: Shapes that stay rough at every zoom level — coastlines and snowflakes whose detail never bottoms out, with dimensions that are not whole numbers.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of sets exhibiting self-similarity and non-integer Hausdorff dimension, often arising as attractors of iterated function systems.
sources: Mandelbrot, The Fractal Geometry of Nature

concepts.name: Projective Geometry
concepts.intuition_gloss: The geometry of perspective drawing, where parallel train tracks really do meet — at horizon points added to the plane.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of properties invariant under projection, on the projective space obtained by adjoining points at infinity; formally, the space of lines through the origin of a vector space.
sources: Princeton Companion (projective geometry as unifying frame)
leverage: unifies the conics, underlies algebraic geometry and computer vision

concepts.name: Erlangen Program (Geometry via Symmetry Groups)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Klein's big idea: a geometry is defined not by its objects but by the moves you are allowed to make — study whatever the moves leave unchanged.
concepts.formal_gloss: Klein's 1872 organizing principle classifying geometries as the study of invariants of a transformation group acting on a space, ordering Euclidean ⊂ affine ⊂ projective by group inclusion.
sources: Klein, Vergleichende Betrachtungen über neuere geometrische Forschungen (1872)
leverage: the single deepest 'what is geometry?' answer; template for modern invariant-based thinking

concepts.name: Curves & Surfaces (Differential Geometry)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Calculus aimed at bent things: describing winding curves and rolling surfaces by how they change from point to point.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of smooth parametrized curves and surfaces in R³ via derivatives — tangent and normal vectors, arc length, and the first and second fundamental forms.
sources: do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

concepts.name: Curvature
concepts.intuition_gloss: A number saying how sharply something bends at each point — zero for flat, positive for sphere-like domes, negative for saddle shapes.
concepts.formal_gloss: For curves, the rate of turning of the tangent per unit arc length; for surfaces, Gaussian curvature, which Gauss's Theorema Egregium shows is intrinsic — detectable by measurements within the surface alone.

resources.title: But why is a sphere's surface area four times its shadow? (3Blue1Brown)

sources: Gauss, Disquisitiones generales circa superficies curvas (1827)
Gauss-Bonnet theorem as bridge to topology
leverage: the pivotal concept linking local calculus to global shape, and geometry to general relativity

concepts.name: Geodesic
concepts.intuition_gloss: The straightest possible path in a curved world — what a stretched string or a long-haul flight route follows.
concepts.formal_gloss: A locally length-minimizing curve, equivalently one whose tangent vector is parallel-transported along itself, satisfying the geodesic differential equation.
sources: do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

concepts.name: Riemannian Geometry
concepts.intuition_gloss: Geometry from the inside: give every point of a space its own tiny ruler, and distance, angle, and curvature all follow — the mathematics of curved universes.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of smooth manifolds equipped with a Riemannian metric — a smoothly varying inner product on tangent spaces — yielding lengths, geodesics, and curvature tensors.
sources: Princeton Companion (Riemannian geometry / Ricci flow chapters)
modern leverage: general relativity, Perelman's proof of the Poincaré conjecture

concepts.name: Algebraic Geometry (Varieties)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Shapes carved out by polynomial equations, studied by letting the algebra of those equations speak for the geometry.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of algebraic varieties — solution sets of systems of polynomial equations — via the commutative rings of functions on them, and their scheme-theoretic generalization.
sources: leverage: engine behind Wiles's proof of Fermat's Last Theorem and modern number theory
